story:
Mouse!
In my house.
I know not whence it came.

Eyes—beady, bright—
glare at me; whiskers chuff.
“Shoo!” I stomp. “Be gone!”
“No,” it squeaks, polite but firm.
“It cannot be done. I’m sorry.”

“What?” I sputter.
“Insolence? In my house?”
Yet the little thing just bows.
“Cheese, please, sir. I beg you.
I am poor and very hungry.”

Cheese. Always cheese.
Pity stirs.
I cut a crumbly, sharp triangle—
cheddar, orange and neat—
and pass it down.
“There. A triangle. Now off with you.”

“Thanks,” it says. “But—my wife.”
A refined lady, it claims.
“Camembert, sir, if you please.”
I slice again.

“And the children,” it adds.
Gouda for Eileen, brie for Maurice.
My knife goes up and down;
my cupboard thins;
the soup I dreamed of cools.
Toast goes bare.

“More,” says the mouse.
“Cousins, aunts, uncles—wedding guests!
Edam, feta, parmesan, gruyere, blue,
add a bit of queso for the kids, a dab of cream.”

I sigh and cut and cut,
each wedge turned into tidy triangles.
“More,” they squeak.
And then—more mice.
From cracks, rafters, shadows:
a tide of fur and whispering feet,
as if millions—no exact number,
just too many to count—swarm my home.
They hang from strings, perch on shelves,
debate the merits of Havarti and Swiss,
call for halloumi, Muenster, cottage by the bowl,
and, above all, that cake of cheese
some folks call dessert.

Sweat stings my eyes.
They cheer and jeer, chant “More!”
They tip my fridge,
hand me a bigger knife,
wheel in a stainless board,
urge me to keep the triangles coming.
Another slice, then diagonal—
two perfect pieces, their favorite shape.

“Another cheesy triangle!”
My arm goes dull; my back, sore.
Still they press.
What if I run out?
Will their hunger stop?
Every blink, more mice.
They gamble on slices, roar when they lose.
“Faster! Harder! More triangles!”

“Mouse!” I plead. “How much more?”
“More and ever more,” he says,
and the crowd applauds.
“I’ve given you an inch—”
“—and we’ll have a mile.”
They like my house, they say.
It would be a shame
to chew the walls.

I agree: better cheese than walls.
“And one small fact,” says the mouse.
“Mice, though small, are fond of meat.”
Silence falls.
Ten thousand—no, who knows—eyes fix on me.
“M–meat?” I whisper.
They lick their lips,
but keep it civil:
“Keep cutting triangles well,
or the cutter may be…recycled.”

My throat dries.
I slice faster, calmer,
hand out mozzarella, a drift of asiago,
as long as I can.

At last: out of cheese.
The fridge, a moon: barren and cold.
“Well,” says the mouse, almost kindly,
“you’ve done quite well. Now we’ll feast—”
“Wait!” I blurt. “A moment!”
Impatient eyebrow. Rumbling bellies.

Think, man.
No more cheese here,
but somewhere—yes.
“Mouse! There is more cheese!”
He scans my empty shelves.
“Not here—at the store.
I’ll fetch my wallet,
drive down,
return with mountains.”

A pause.
A nod.
They agree.

I flee.
Across town, across the sea—
as far as small legs can’t follow.
And my house?
For sale. As-is.
Good bones. Freshly swept.
Some tiny guests may linger.
Bring plenty of cheese.
